questions.text: If a question says 'evaluate and justify', addressing only the evaluation is enough for full marks.
questions.explanation: In 2024, many students did not note the two command terms 'evaluate' and 'justify' and lost marks.
questions.studyTip: Tick off each command term - evaluate AND justify.

questions.text: When a question uses two command terms, such as evaluate and justify, you must address both.
questions.explanation: Command terms define what the answer must do; missing one forfeits those marks.
questions.studyTip: Underline the command words before you start.

concepts.title: Answer this scenario, not a past one

questions.text: Reusing a well-rehearsed answer from a past exam question is a reliable way to score.
questions.explanation: In 2024, responses that rewrote past-exam ideas did not fit the year's scenarios and scored poorly.
questions.studyTip: Answer this year's scenario, not last year's question.

questions.text: Answers must address the specific scenario presented, not rewritten ideas from past exams.
questions.explanation: Each scenario is unique; a pre-planned answer rarely fits.
questions.studyTip: Engage with the exact stimulus given.

questions.text: When discussing data such as wind speed or energy output, you can describe the trend without stating units.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students discussing wind data often omitted m/s or kWh and lost marks.
questions.studyTip: Numbers plus units (m/s, kWh) every time.

questions.text: You should quote specific figures with their units from the data provided.
questions.explanation: Specific, correctly-unitted figures show you can read and use the data.
questions.studyTip: Cite the value and its unit.

concepts.title: Answer the exact focus of the question

questions.text: As long as your answer is about the right topic, it does not matter which specific part of the question you address.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students asked about 'grid reliability' instead discussed general efficiency or transmission loss.
questions.studyTip: Answer the precise focus, not the general topic.

questions.text: You must address the specific focus of the question (e.g. grid reliability), not a related general point.
questions.explanation: Marks are tied to the exact thing asked.
questions.studyTip: Re-read the last line of the question - that's the focus.

concepts.title: Give one clear idea when asked

questions.text: When a question asks for a single reason, listing several possible options is a safe approach.
questions.explanation: In 2023, examiners advised giving a clear single idea rather than several possible points for such questions.

questions.text: When one clear idea is required, give a single well-developed point rather than a list.
questions.explanation: A list of possibilities can read as uncertainty and may not be credited.
questions.studyTip: Commit to one idea and develop it.

questions.text: Restating the information in the question stem is enough to demonstrate understanding.
questions.explanation: Across years, restating terms such as 'warming potential' without explanation scored poorly.
questions.studyTip: Don't echo the stem - add explanation.

questions.text: You must explain beyond the stem and apply the relevant term to the example given.
questions.explanation: Marks come from applied reasoning, not repetition of the prompt.
questions.studyTip: Define, then apply to the scenario.

questions.text: Accuracy, precision, validity and repeatability all describe much the same quality of data.
questions.explanation: Confusing these terms is flagged as a common error in every report from 2022 to 2025.

questions.text: Accuracy, precision, validity and repeatability are distinct terms and must be applied correctly.
questions.explanation: Accuracy is closeness to the true value; precision is closeness of repeats; validity is measuring the intended thing.

questions.text: An incorrectly calibrated instrument is an example of a personal (random) error.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2024, students jumbled error types; incorrect calibration is a systematic error.

questions.text: An incorrectly calibrated instrument causes a systematic error; misreading a device is a personal error.
questions.explanation: Systematic errors shift every reading the same way; personal errors are one-off mistakes.

questions.text: Repeating a measurement several times makes an investigation valid.
questions.explanation: In 2025, group B measured turbidity five times to improve repeatability/reliability, not validity.

questions.text: Repeating measurements improves reliability; validity is whether you measured what you intended.
questions.explanation: Validity depends on method design, not how many times you repeat.

questions.text: The variable the researcher measures as an outcome is the independent variable.
questions.explanation: In 2022, many students mis-identified the dependent variable.

questions.text: The independent variable is deliberately changed; the dependent variable is the measured outcome.
questions.explanation: Plot the IV on the x-axis and the DV on the y-axis.
questions.studyTip: Change the IV, measure the DV.

questions.text: Counts of organisms recorded as numbers are an example of qualitative data.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2024, students wrongly labelled numerical (quantitative) data as qualitative.

questions.text: Number-based measurements are quantitative; qualitative data are descriptive and non-numerical.
questions.explanation: Counts, percentages and concentrations are quantitative.

questions.text: Simpson's Index of Diversity is just the sum of the squared proportions of each species.
questions.explanation: In 2025, some responses did not subtract the sum from 1 and were incomplete.

questions.text: Simpson's Index of Diversity is found by subtracting the sum of squared proportions from 1.
questions.explanation: SID = 1 - the sum of (proportion of each species) squared.

questions.text: A higher Simpson's Index of Diversity simply means a greater total number of species.
questions.explanation: In 2023, 'the total number of species' was a common incorrect interpretation of the index.
questions.studyTip: It's not just a species count.

questions.text: A higher index indicates greater diversity (richness and evenness); dominance by one species lowers it.
questions.explanation: Both the number of species and how evenly individuals are spread affect the index.

questions.text: To find percentage change you divide the new value by the old value and multiply by 100.
questions.explanation: In 2025, the correct working was (421 - 329) / 329 x 100, about 28 per cent.
questions.studyTip: Use the difference over the original.

questions.text: Percentage change is the difference (new minus old), divided by the old value, times 100.
questions.explanation: Always divide the change by the original value, not the new one.

concepts.title: Describe data with figures and the right interval

questions.text: Describing a graph as showing 'a rapid increase' is sufficient analysis of the data.
questions.explanation: In 2025, responses describing the whole graph or saying 'rapid increase' without figures lost marks.
questions.studyTip: Quote figures for the asked interval.

questions.text: Describe the change with specific years and figures, focusing on the interval the question specifies.
questions.explanation: Targeted, quantified description shows real data analysis.

questions.text: Finding high PFAS levels in animals that died shows that PFAS caused their deaths.
questions.explanation: In 2024, the key limitation was that the study did not establish a causal link between PFAS levels and the pups' deaths.
questions.studyTip: A link is not proof of cause.

questions.text: A correlation between a chemical's level and deaths does not establish cause and effect; other factors may be involved.
questions.explanation: Establishing causation needs a controlled design that rules out other explanations.

questions.text: 'Threatened' is the IUCN Red List category for a species at moderate risk.
questions.explanation: In 2025, 'threatened' (not an IUCN category) and over-stating to 'endangered' were common errors.

questions.text: IUCN categories include vulnerable, endangered and critically endangered, in increasing order of risk.
questions.explanation: Match the category to the evidence of risk; don't over-state to endangered without cause.

questions.text: A population with low genetic diversity resists new disease just as well as a diverse one.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, better responses linked genetic diversity to resilience and resistance to disease.

questions.text: Low genetic diversity reduces the chance a population has natural resistance to new disease or change.
questions.explanation: Variation means some individuals may survive a new threat, sustaining the population.
questions.studyTip: More genetic variation = better odds of surviving change.

questions.text: An endemic species is one that has been introduced to a region from somewhere else.
questions.explanation: In 2024, recognising the Australian sea lion as endemic (found only here) was often missed.

questions.text: An endemic species occurs naturally in only one particular region and nowhere else.
questions.explanation: Endemism makes a species more vulnerable, as its whole range is limited.
questions.studyTip: Endemic = native to one place only.

questions.text: The background extinction rate is the rate of extinctions caused by a major catastrophic event.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students lacking a clear understanding of 'background extinction rate' chose wrong options.

questions.text: The background extinction rate is the natural, ongoing rate of extinction without unusual events.
questions.explanation: Mass extinctions are spikes well above this baseline rate.
questions.studyTip: Background = normal; mass extinction = a spike.

questions.text: The main threat an introduced fish poses to a native fish is usually that it is eaten as prey.
questions.explanation: In 2025, describing predation on the native perch by introduced fish (rather than the real threat) was a common error.
questions.studyTip: Watch the direction of the threat.

questions.text: Introduced species threaten natives through competition, predation on them, and habitat disruption.
questions.explanation: The native is usually the one out-competed or preyed upon, not the predator.
questions.studyTip: Introduced species compete with / prey on natives.

questions.text: Bioaccumulation is the increase in toxin concentration from one trophic level to the next up a food chain.
questions.explanation: In 2024, confusing bioaccumulation and biomagnification led many to the wrong option.
questions.studyTip: Accumulate within; magnify up the chain.

questions.text: Bioaccumulation is build-up of a substance within one organism over time; biomagnification is the increase up the food chain.
questions.explanation: Both involve toxins, but at different scales - one organism vs the whole chain.
questions.studyTip: Accumulate = one organism; magnify = food chain.

concepts.title: One site cannot prove a global trend

questions.text: Temperature data from a single site is enough to conclude whether global warming is occurring.
questions.explanation: In 2024, a common misconception was that single-site data can evidence global warming.

questions.text: Data from one site cannot establish a global trend; broad, long-term, multi-site data is required.
questions.explanation: Local variation can mask or mimic the global signal.
questions.studyTip: One site is not the globe.

questions.text: An ecosystem dominated by one very abundant species has high species diversity.
questions.explanation: In 2025, high-scoring responses noted poor relative abundance (e.g. 25 of one grass vs 2 of others) meant low diversity.

questions.text: Dominance by a single species (low evenness) means lower diversity, even if other species are present.
questions.explanation: Diversity needs both a range of species and reasonably even abundance.

questions.text: Similar fossil species found across different regions are best explained by separate, unrelated origins.
questions.explanation: In 2024, 'similar fossil species across regions' related to rapid diversification from a common ancestor.
questions.studyTip: Similar forms often share an ancestor.

questions.text: Similar fossils across regions can reflect rapid diversification from a common ancestor as ecosystems change.
questions.explanation: Organisms increase from a common ancestor and adapt as conditions change.
questions.studyTip: Rapid diversification = many forms from one ancestor.

questions.text: Ecocentrism is the view that the focus should be on protecting living things such as flora and fauna.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, describing ecocentrism as 'protecting flora and fauna' (a biocentric idea) was a common error.

questions.text: Ecocentrism values all living and non-living parts of ecosystems and de-emphasises human superiority; valuing only living things is biocentrism.
questions.explanation: Ecocentrism covers ecosystems, natural processes and abiotic components, not just organisms.
questions.studyTip: Ecocentric = whole system; biocentric = living things.

questions.text: Valuing the environment only because it benefits humans is an ecocentric worldview.
questions.explanation: In 2025, valuing the environment for human benefit was correctly identified as anthropocentric.

questions.text: Valuing the environment for human benefit is anthropocentric; valuing land, water, plants and animals for themselves goes beyond it.
questions.explanation: Considering oneself part of, not superior to, nature moves beyond anthropocentrism.

questions.text: Intergenerational equity means sharing environmental resources fairly among people of the present generation.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused intra- and inter-generational equity.

questions.text: Intragenerational equity is fairness within the present generation; intergenerational equity is fairness for future generations.
questions.explanation: Intra is 'within today'; inter is 'across generations'.
questions.studyTip: Intra = within today; inter = future generations.

concepts.title: Don't pass costs to future generations

questions.text: If past mining leaves clean-up costs for today's community, that demonstrates intergenerational equity.
questions.explanation: In 2024, gold extraction before the 1920s left repair costs for the current generation - a failure of intergenerational equity.

questions.text: Leaving environmental clean-up costs for a later generation is a failure of intergenerational equity.
questions.explanation: Equity means each generation does not burden the next with its damage.
questions.studyTip: Don't dump your costs on the future.

questions.text: The precautionary principle simply means being cautious and trying not to damage the environment.
questions.explanation: In 2022, applying the principle as vaguely 'being cautious' scored poorly.

questions.text: The precautionary principle says a lack of full scientific certainty should not delay action to prevent serious or irreversible harm.
questions.explanation: It applies specifically where potential harm is serious or irreversible.
questions.studyTip: Uncertainty is no excuse for inaction on serious harm.

questions.text: The EPBC Act sets out the specific strategies to conserve a particular species and its habitat.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students wrongly said the EPBC Act outlines specific conservation strategies for the wattle.
questions.studyTip: It provides legal protection, not a recovery plan.

questions.text: The EPBC Act provides legal protection by requiring assessment and approval of actions affecting listed species.
questions.explanation: Tailored recovery actions sit in separate plans, not the Act itself.
questions.studyTip: EPBC Act = legal protection and assessment.

questions.text: CITES is the appropriate tool for protecting a single local national park.
questions.explanation: In 2025, using CITES to protect a local park was inappropriate.

questions.text: CITES is an international treaty regulating trade in endangered species, not a tool for local park management.
questions.explanation: Local protection comes from national or state law, not a trade treaty.
questions.studyTip: CITES = international trade in endangered species.

questions.text: A seed bank and a gene bank are the same thing.
questions.explanation: In 2022, responses that confused a seed bank with a gene bank did not score well.

questions.text: A seed bank stores seeds, whereas a gene bank stores genetic material more broadly (seeds, tissues, gametes).
questions.explanation: All seed banks are a form of gene bank, but gene banks are broader.
questions.studyTip: Seed bank = seeds; gene bank = genetic material.

questions.text: Base load energy is the extra supply brought online to meet short periods of highest demand.
questions.explanation: Base load was poorly understood across years; it is the continuous demand, not the peaks.
questions.studyTip: Base load is constant, not the spikes.

questions.text: Base load is the continuous minimum demand met at all times; peak load is the extra for periods of highest demand.
questions.explanation: Base load must be linked to a continuous supply over time.
questions.studyTip: Base = constant minimum; peak = the spikes.

questions.text: A resource is renewable if there is simply a very large amount of it available to use.
questions.explanation: In 2024, the Sun as a source that 'cannot be depleted' defined renewable.

questions.text: A renewable resource is replenished at a higher rate than it is consumed, such as solar energy.
questions.explanation: Renewability is about the rate of replenishment versus use.

questions.text: The energy stored in biomass is gravitational potential energy.
questions.explanation: In 2025, describing biomass energy as 'potential' was not specific enough; it is chemical energy.

questions.text: Biomass stores chemical energy, which is released when it is burnt.
questions.explanation: The chemical energy originated from sunlight captured by photosynthesis.

questions.text: Burning bioethanol or biomass releases no carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2025, students missed that bioethanol combustion (and fermentation) does produce CO2.

questions.text: Burning biofuels releases CO2, but it is more carbon-neutral because the plants absorbed CO2 while growing.
questions.explanation: Carbon cycles in the short term: absorbed in growth, released on burning.

questions.text: Nuclear power is best suited to supplying short peak-load periods of demand.
questions.explanation: In 2025, nuclear was identified as suitable for base load for a large population.

questions.text: Nuclear power provides a steady output well suited to base load, not rapidly variable peak load.
questions.explanation: Nuclear plants are not easily ramped up and down quickly.

questions.text: Pumped hydro storage can be built almost anywhere, regardless of water access or terrain.
questions.explanation: In 2022, the reason to choose batteries over pumped hydro was access to water and a suitable site.

questions.text: Pumped hydro needs a suitable site with water and elevation; large batteries are chosen where these are unavailable.
questions.explanation: Storage choice depends on geography and resources.
questions.studyTip: Pumped hydro needs water + height; batteries are flexible.

questions.text: Burning coal mainly releases water vapour, with little carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: In 2024, better responses clearly stated that coal combustion releases carbon dioxide.

questions.text: The combustion of coal releases carbon dioxide, contributing to the enhanced greenhouse effect.
questions.explanation: Burning fossil fuels adds previously-stored carbon to the atmosphere.

questions.text: To compare how much energy people use in two countries, compare each country's total energy use.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students who used total energy rather than per-person figures chose the wrong option.

questions.text: Comparing energy use fairly requires energy use per person, not just total national use.
questions.explanation: A large country may use more in total simply because it has more people.

questions.text: The enhanced greenhouse effect occurs because greenhouse gases trap incoming infrared radiation from the Sun.
questions.explanation: In 2025, lower-scoring responses said warming is caused by incoming solar infrared, rather than visible light re-emitted as infrared.
questions.studyTip: Sun in = visible; Earth out = infrared.

questions.text: Incoming solar radiation is largely visible light; Earth re-radiates it as infrared, which greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit.
questions.explanation: It is the re-radiated infrared, not incoming light, that greenhouse gases absorb.
questions.studyTip: Visible in, infrared out (absorbed by GHGs).

questions.text: Saying greenhouse gases 'act like a blanket keeping Earth warm' is a complete explanation of the greenhouse effect.
questions.explanation: In 2022, 'gases like a blanket' or 'trapping light' lacked the detail required.
questions.studyTip: Name the radiation, not a blanket.

questions.text: A full explanation describes gases absorbing and re-emitting re-radiated infrared, keeping heat near the surface.
questions.explanation: Use the terms infrared radiation, absorb and re-emit, not analogies.

concepts.title: Ozone depletion is not global warming

questions.text: The hole in the ozone layer is the main cause of global warming.
questions.explanation: In 2022, connecting the ozone 'hole' with global warming was flagged as an incorrect understanding.

questions.text: Ozone depletion and the enhanced greenhouse effect are separate issues with different causes.
questions.explanation: Ozone loss is mainly about UV; warming is about infrared and greenhouse gases.

questions.text: A gas's global warming potential simply means how much of that gas is present in the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2025, 'warming potential' was often restated as amount or lifetime rather than heat-trapping ability.
questions.studyTip: GWP is about heat trapped, not quantity.

questions.text: Global warming potential measures how much heat a gas traps relative to carbon dioxide over a given time.
questions.explanation: It compares a gas's warming effect to that of CO2.

questions.text: Carbon sequestration works by filtering carbon dioxide out of the atmosphere and storing it in the ocean.
questions.explanation: In 2022, the key was capturing CO2 before it enters the atmosphere, not filtering the atmosphere.

questions.text: Sequestration captures CO2 before it enters the atmosphere, or stores it in sinks such as plants and soil.
questions.explanation: Carbon capture targets emissions at the source; natural sinks store carbon biologically.
questions.studyTip: Capture before release; store in sinks.

questions.text: The confidence rating of a climate projection states how accurate and valid the projection is.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused confidence with 'how accurate or valid'; it is about likelihood.

questions.text: A confidence rating expresses how plausible or likely the projected range is for a given emission scenario.
questions.explanation: It reflects the strength of evidence and agreement, not measured accuracy.

questions.text: Building a sea wall to cope with rising sea levels is an example of climate change mitigation.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused mitigation and adaptation strategies.
questions.studyTip: A sea wall copes with impacts = adaptation.

questions.text: Mitigation reduces greenhouse gas emissions or causes; adaptation adjusts to impacts (e.g. a sea wall).
questions.explanation: Mitigation tackles the cause; adaptation manages the consequences.
questions.studyTip: Mitigate = reduce causes; adapt = cope with effects.

questions.text: Cities are warmer than surrounding areas mainly because they contain more vegetation.
questions.explanation: In 2024, better responses linked darker city surfaces absorbing more radiation to the urban heat island effect.

questions.text: The urban heat island effect arises because dark city surfaces absorb more radiation (reflect less) than lighter ones.
questions.explanation: Less vegetation and more dark, sealed surfaces raise city temperatures.
questions.studyTip: Dark surfaces absorb more -> cities heat up.

questions.text: Adding vertical gardens and green roofs to a city has no effect on carbon dioxide levels.
questions.explanation: In 2024, better responses noted the plants absorb CO2 when photosynthesising, aiding local sequestration.

questions.text: Plants in green roofs and vertical gardens absorb CO2 during photosynthesis, assisting localised carbon sequestration.
questions.explanation: Vegetation provides a local carbon sink as well as cooling.
questions.studyTip: Photosynthesis removes CO2 - green infrastructure helps.
